attributes.stem: An 89-year-old man attends his general practitioner for a general health check. His current health issues include heart failure with reduced ejection fraction and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. He is wheelchair-bound and quite breathless at rest. His jugular venous pressure is raised and he has crepitations at both lung bases. There is ankle oedema extending up to mid-calf. During the physical examination the doctor notices an 8 mm lesion on back of the patient's right hand (Image). The patient states that the lesion has been there for about a year, has not changed at all and does not bother him.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
attributes.explanation:
This lesion is almost certainly a nodular basal cell carcinoma (BCC). It is raised and pearly white - two characteristics of a BCC. Whilst the general advice for these lesions is formal local excision, a good case can be made for conservative management with watchful waiting, particularly in older patients and those with significant co-morbidities. This patient has severe cardio-pulmonary disease and probably a relatively limited outlook. For low risk lesions and those smaller than 1 cm, when sited on the trunk or extremities, a plan of active conservative management can be promoted.

Imiquimod is a immune-response modifier, most commonly utilised for the treatment of superficial (rather than nodular) BCCs <2cm in diameter, which usually results in minimal scarring of the affected area. As such, this option would be less appropriate.

Cryotherapy is most often reserved for small, superficial BCCs which cover areas on the trunk or limbs. This patients age and co-morbidites, along with the fact this patient appears unbothered by his lesion make this a less appealing option

Excision with a 1 cm margin would be less appropriate in this patient, as his likelihood of healing well with this degree of excision is significantly diminished due to his age, and the likely corticosteroid treatment he is on for his COPD.
learningPoints: Watchful waiting is an appropriate management option for BCCs in older patients, or those with significant co-morbidities.
references: Linos  E, Chren M-M. Active Surveillance as a Management Option for Low-risk Basal Cell Carcinoma. JAMA Int Med 2021; 181:1032-333.
